parentQuestion: During childbirth, cervical stretch triggers oxytocin release, which intensifies contractions, causing more cervical stretch and more oxytocin release. This cycle accelerates until delivery occurs. A medical student asks, 'Why doesn't this kill the mother by continuing indefinitely?' What's the answer?

options: A mechanism that amplifies the original stimulus
A mechanism that stops all change
scaffolds.explain: POSITIVE FEEDBACK is a mechanism that AMPLIFIES the original stimulus. Unlike negative feedback which opposes change, positive feedback accelerates it.

wrongExplains.0: Opposing change is negative feedback, not positive.
wrongExplains.2: Maintaining balance is homeostasis via negative feedback.
wrongExplains.3: Positive feedback accelerates change rather than stopping it.

scaffolds.q: What happens during childbirth that exemplifies positive feedback?
options: Contractions slow down to protect the mother
Cervical stretch triggers oxytocin, causing stronger contractions
scaffolds.explain: During childbirth, CERVICAL STRETCH triggers OXYTOCIN release, causing STRONGER CONTRACTIONS. Each contraction leads to more stretch and even more oxytocin.

wrongExplains.0: Contractions intensify during labor, not slow down.
wrongExplains.3: Pain doesn't stop contractions; the cycle continues.

scaffolds.q: What would happen if positive feedback continued forever?
options: The stimulus would disappear on its own
scaffolds.explain: If positive feedback continued forever, THE SYSTEM WOULD BE DESTROYED. That's why positive feedback loops MUST have an endpoint.

wrongExplains.0: Unchecked positive feedback is dangerous, not adaptive.
wrongExplains.2: Positive feedback doesn't convert to negative feedback.
wrongExplains.3: Positive feedback amplifies the stimulus, not makes it disappear.

scaffolds.q: What terminates the positive feedback loop in childbirth?
scaffolds.explain: DELIVERY OF THE BABY terminates the loop. Once the baby is born, CERVICAL STRETCH STOPS, ending the trigger for oxytocin release.

wrongExplains.0: Pain receptors don't end the loop; delivery does.
wrongExplains.2: Exhaustion doesn't stop the feedback mechanism.
wrongExplains.3: Oxytocin continues until cervical stretch stops.

scaffolds.q: Why do positive feedback loops need external termination events?
options: The loop can't stop itself — something external must end it
scaffolds.explain: Positive feedback loops CAN'T STOP THEMSELVES — something EXTERNAL must end them. The self-amplifying nature prevents internal termination.

wrongExplains.0: Positive feedback is self-amplifying, not self-limiting.
wrongExplains.2: Termination is about system survival, not just pain.
wrongExplains.3: Termination is physiologically necessary, not just comfort.

scaffolds.q: How does delivery stop the childbirth feedback loop?
scaffolds.explain: Delivery REMOVES THE STIMULUS — no more cervical stretch means no more oxytocin trigger, so contractions stop.

wrongExplains.0: Delivery decreases oxytocin by removing the stimulus.
wrongExplains.2: Delivery ends contractions, doesn't cause more.
wrongExplains.3: Removal of stimulus, not new hormones, ends the loop.

scaffolds.q: What distinguishes positive from negative feedback?
options: Positive feedback amplifies while negative feedback opposes change
scaffolds.explain: POSITIVE FEEDBACK AMPLIFIES while NEGATIVE FEEDBACK OPPOSES change. Positive accelerates, negative stabilizes.

wrongExplains.0: Negative feedback maintains homeostasis; positive disrupts it temporarily.
wrongExplains.3: Negative feedback opposes change, not amplifies.

scaffolds.q: Why doesn't the childbirth feedback loop kill the mother?
options: Delivery provides an external termination event
scaffolds.explain: DELIVERY provides an EXTERNAL TERMINATION EVENT. The loop ends because the stimulus (cervical stretch) is removed when the baby is born.

wrongExplains.0: Pain doesn't terminate the feedback mechanism.
wrongExplains.2: Desensitization doesn't stop the loop; delivery does.
wrongExplains.3: Homeostasis can't override an ongoing positive feedback loop.

scaffolds.q: What's the answer to why childbirth's positive feedback doesn't continue indefinitely?
options: The mother's body adapts to oxytocin
Positive feedback requires an external termination event (delivery)
scaffolds.explain: The answer: POSITIVE FEEDBACK REQUIRES AN EXTERNAL TERMINATION EVENT. Delivery removes cervical stretch, ending the oxytocin trigger.

wrongExplains.0: The loop ends due to delivery, not adaptation.
wrongExplains.2: Negative feedback doesn't override; the stimulus is removed.
wrongExplains.3: The mechanism ends from delivery, not muscle fatigue.
